notes: AMD adopted GHz equivalency model numbering for Athlon XP processors naming models with clock-speed GHz ratings claimed equivalent to Intel Pentium 4 based on application benchmarks
AMD Athlon XP 2000+ processor runs at 1.667GHz actual clock speed but carries model number claiming equivalence to Intel P4 at 2.0GHz Willamette
Tests run on P4 at 1.5-1.8GHz and Athlon XP at 1.33-1.53GHz with P4 1.5GHz as 100% normalization point
SYSmark 2001 accounts for slightly more than one-third of entire performance score used to derive GHz equivalency; one half of Office Productivity and two-thirds of Content Creation are SYSmark-based
No SYSmark 2001 results for Athlon XP 2000+ were reported to BAPCo as of Feb 15 2002 despite BAPCo rules requiring public disclosure of all published results
Numerous small discrepancies between SYSmark 2001 results in AMD white paper and BAPCo site suggesting repeatability problems or premature publication

AMD and Microsoft found and fixed Media Player bug benefiting Athlon instructions; AMD included results both with and without patch a situation no real user would have; found after Arthur Andersen audit
Winbench 2000 measures graphics using DirectX 7.0 but all systems tested with Windows XP which ships with DirectX 8.1; using obsolete API version is unrealistic
Intel own scores at BAPCo beat AMD-reported results for same Intel processor on SYSmark 2001 and Quake Demo Arena II; lower AMD-on-Intel results artificially inflated AMD equivalency
Flaw 1: GHz equivalency implies fixed relationship over time but time-dependency makes ratings increasingly invalid as software benchmarks and hardware evolve
Flaw 2: Methodology assumes specific workload usage model (one-third office one-third content creation one-third gaming) that may not apply to many users
Flaw 3: Methodology mixes system I/O performance with processor-intensive work; Business Winstone 2001 measures system-level performance not processor alone
Flaw 4: GHz equivalency is totally platform dependent; chipsets memory architecture device drivers and other platform components affect results but are attributed to processor
Intel delivered P4 Northwood in January 2002 with same 2.0GHz but significantly better performance due to larger cache and 130nm process; all subsequent P4s built on this base
Athlon XP 2000+ matched against old Willamette P4 that no one was buying while significantly faster Northwood was available at same clock speed
AMD workload characterization assumes usage model in three equal parts: one-third Office Productivity one-third Content Creation one-third 3D Gaming
AMD announced True Performance Initiative (TPI) in October 2001 as strategic effort to develop new means of rating processor performance beyond GHz equivalency
Aberdeen in serious disagreement with AMD over basic methodology believing it fundamentally flawed in measuring processor apples and I/Oranges using arguable workload mix
Close reading of PC trade press and performance web sites shows increasing skepticism toward GHz equivalency; Aberdeen would not be surprised to see press turn surly
Aberdeen recommends AMD examine SPECcpu 2000 as more appropriate processor benchmark before going too far down the True Performance Initiative road
Arthur Andersen audited AMD benchmark methodology using AICPA attestation standards; Media Player bug fix found after audit yet improved numbers still used
Aberdeen predicts AMD will undoubtedly pay a marketing price in 2002 for bad science that confused market and must soon retreat from GHz equivalency
Aberdeen doubts 2002 will pass without new performance measurement methodology from AMD requiring wholesale readjustment in model numbers past present and future
TPI progress needs to be announced soon or initiative will appear to be marketing ploy; TPI has sunk beneath wave of performance-equivalency press releases

metric_value: model numbers reported as actual GHz
notes: European advertisements and web sites reporting GHz-equivalent AMD model numbers as actual GHz ratings; deceptive at best and downright illegal at worst
Equivalency comparisons will only get worse over time as benchmarks operating systems and applications evolve; distinct Pinocchio factor that will grow
AMD processors are quite efficient for lots of applications and do not need steroids of hokey equivalency to deserve market respect
GHz alone is meaningless indicator of performance; only measures how often processor clock ticks and says nothing about how much work gets done per tick

metric_value: feinting on GHz while hoping for up-clocking
notes: Some cynics believe AMD real strategy is feinting one way by arguing GHz meaningless while hoping market will conflate model numbers with actual GHz
AMD Athlon XP model numbers: 1500+ at 1.33GHz 1600+ at 1.4GHz 1700+ at 1.47GHz 1800+ at 1.53GHz 2000+ at 1.667GHz actual clock speeds
Arthur Andersen dissolved in 2002 following Enron scandal undermining credibility of their attestation of AMD benchmark methodology
AMD abandoned GHz equivalency when launching Athlon 64 in September 2003 using model numbers without explicit GHz equivalency claims vindicating Aberdeen predictions
Entire industry eventually moved beyond GHz as primary marketing metric during multi-core era validating Aberdeen fundamental point that clock speed is poor performance indicator
AMD adopted Zen architecture and became major competitor to Intel validating that AMD products were strong enough without misleading GHz equivalency marketing
Ziff Davis Winstone 2001 replaced with Winstone 2002 during analysis period; freezing benchmark suites unrealistic but updating them changes equivalency metric
AMD stated purpose was providing scientific basis for what would otherwise be benchmarketing; Aberdeen argued AMD failed at their own stated goal of scientific rigor
